A group adopts these premises as fixed and tries to evaluate whether a system creates knowledge.

They begin with the Einstein benchmark. Because the benchmark’s question has been curated by history as worth solving, success on it cannot straightforwardly show how the system identifies worthwhile questions in the first place. The benchmark may therefore hide an important part of cognition: not only finding answers, but selecting problems before history has validated them.

This immediately destabilizes the metaphor that intelligence works by collapsing search space. If the benchmark already contains a historically selected target, then part of the search has been pre-compressed by culture. The remaining task may look like monotonic narrowing only because prior generations already performed the expansive phase. So the group concludes that creative progress may require alternation: first expansion into many candidate framings, then compression onto one; then expansion again when the framing proves insufficient. Search is not simply reduced. It may be rhythmically widened and narrowed.

They then test an edge case. The system produces a novel explanation. Humans initially reject it. Given the premises, the framework they are using cannot determine what happened. Three live possibilities remain: the system created knowledge that humans failed to recognize; the system had knowledge-like content but communicated it badly; or the humans, acting as evaluators, were themselves limited. The framework does not adjudicate among these. So the group cannot treat initial rejection as decisive evidence against knowledge creation.

At this point, the last two premises remain: “Punctuist clairvoyancy taglioni bernese beguiler” and “Medicaids barbedness nonrudimentariness isotimic relics.” Since these are also axioms, the group must accommodate them. But they do not come with an interpretation. They might be meaningful in a code the group lacks, or they might function only as strings that resist integration. Their presence sharpens the earlier problem: if an output is novel and not understood, the framework cannot say whether the issue lies in the output, its transmission, or the evaluators. These two sentences become a limiting case of the rejected-explanation problem.

So the world implied by the premises is one in which evaluation is underdetermined. Historically curated benchmarks can conceal the expansive stages of inquiry. Creative progress may oscillate between generating possibilities and pruning them. And when unfamiliar outputs appear—especially ones humans reject or cannot parse—the existing framework cannot cleanly sort knowledge creation from communication failure or evaluator inadequacy.

The result is uncomfortable but precise: the observers cannot rely on benchmark success, cannot rely on a simple compression metaphor, and cannot rely on immediate human acceptance as the final test of whether something new has been known.
